core_meaning: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is a remarkably versatile word that works as an adjective meaning 'fine / splendid / good enough,' as an adverb meaning 'quite / fairly / more than expected,' and as a polite refusal meaning 'no thank you / I'm good.' The key thread is a sense of something meeting or exceeding a threshold — either positively ('that's great') or neutrally ('that's sufficient, I don't need more').

sentences.jp: 「もう<ruby>少<rt>すこ</rt></ruby>し<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる？」「いや、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です。」
sentences.translation: 'Do you want a little more to eat?' 'No, I'm fine, thank you.'
sentences.notes: This is the classic polite-refusal use of <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です — it declines an offer without the bluntness of いいえ or いりません.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かったよ。
sentences.translation: That movie was actually pretty interesting, you know.
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> works as an adverb meaning 'quite' or 'more than I expected' — a very common everyday use among friends.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>大変<rt>たいへん</rt></ruby>だって<ruby>分<rt>わ</rt></ruby>かった。
sentences.translation: I realized this job is actually pretty tough.
sentences.notes: The adverb sense again, showing that <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> often carries a nuance of mild surprise — 'more so than I assumed.'

sentences.jp: この<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>のインテリアは<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>なものですね。
sentences.translation: The interior of this room is truly splendid.
sentences.notes: This is the more classical/formal adjectival use of <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> meaning 'splendid' or 'excellent' — less common in daily speech but still heard in polite compliments.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>店<rt>みせ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>値段<rt>ねだん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>いから<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>をつけてね。
sentences.translation: Prices at that shop are pretty high, so watch out.
sentences.notes: A natural casual warning using <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> as 'quite' — shows it collocates freely with adjectives like <ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>い, <ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しい, <ruby>遠<rt>とお</rt></ruby>い, etc.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です as a refusal in very casual situations with close friends — it can sound unexpectedly stiff; just say いい or いらない instead. Also avoid using the 'splendid' adjectival sense in casual speech, as it will sound old-fashioned or overly formal. Be careful that <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です is genuinely ambiguous — depending on intonation and context it can mean either 'yes, that's great' or 'no thank you,' so learners sometimes cause confusion.

confused_with.contrast: Both <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> and かなり mean 'quite / fairly,' but かなり is more straightforwardly emphatic — it simply means 'considerably' with no nuance of surprise. <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> often implies 'more than I expected,' giving it a slightly warmer or more conversational feel. For example, かなり<ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しい sounds more objectively strong ('considerably difficult'), while <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しい feels more like a personal, slightly surprised assessment.

frequency: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers. The adverb sense ('quite/fairly') is ubiquitous in daily conversation. The polite-refusal sense is essential for everyday social interaction. The classical 'splendid' adjectival sense appears mostly in formal speech or writing.
anki_hint: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> has three lives: adverb ('quite/more than expected'), polite refusal ('I'm good, no thanks'), and formal adjective ('splendid') — context and intonation tell you which one.
